re: Response to Good-Faith Letter of April 7, 2026
Johnson v. Johnson, Civil Action No. 2025CV936

body.text: I am in receipt of your correspondence dated April 7, 2026, captioned as a "final good-faith effort" to resolve alleged discovery deficiencies. I respond as follows.
I. Production Was Timely Made

On March 26, 2026, I served supplemental discovery responses and a comprehensive production of documents on your office via email to jenn@mcghanlaw.com. That production included a shared Google Drive folder containing all responsive financial documentation, including Navy Federal Credit Union account statements (August 2025 through March 2026), financial summary exhibits (WRJ-001 through WRJ-008), SSDI records, physician-directed medical protocols, and complete technical documentation for the BONDING application.

This production was made within the timeline I communicated to your office on March 19, 2026, when I acknowledged receipt of the Second Request for Production and committed to responding on or before March 26. I fulfilled that commitment.
II. Objections Are Specific and Legally Grounded

Your letter characterizes my responses as "blanket refusals, redactions, and incomplete disclosures." This characterization is inaccurate. My Response and Objections to the Second Request for Production addressed each of the fifteen (15) requests individually and asserted specific, enumerated legal grounds for each objection, including:
(a) AI-generated chat logs classified as cognitive prosthetic output under the ADA and constitutionally protected thought processes;
(b) P31 Labs source code protected as proprietary trade secrets of a separately incorporated Georgia nonprofit corporation;
(c) Psychotherapy notes subject to heightened protection under 45 CFR §164.508(a)(2); and
(d) Requests exceeding the scope of relevance under O.C.G.A. § 9-11-26(b)(1).
These are recognized privileges under Georgia and federal law. The assertion of specific, good-faith objections is neither obstruction nor noncompliance.
III. Financial Records Were Produced

Your letter states that I have "failed to produce critical financial documentation, including but not limited to income records and bank statements." This is incorrect. The March 26 production included eight months of Navy Federal Credit Union statements and a detailed financial summary exhibit. These records confirm what has been consistently represented to the Court: I have zero income, zero liquid assets, and am currently receiving SNAP benefits and Medicaid. There is no undisclosed financial information because there is nothing to disclose.
IV. Motion for Protective Order Is Pending

On April 10, 2026, I filed a Motion for Protective Order Regarding Discovery (PeachCourt Filing #E-NLC3ERUM) addressing the scope of certain requests that implicate trade secrets, medical privacy, and constitutionally protected materials. That motion is now before the Court. I respectfully submit that the appropriate forum for resolving any remaining discovery disputes is before Judge Scarlett, not through escalating correspondence.
V. The Documentary Record Speaks for Itself
For clarity, the complete timeline of discovery communications in this matter is as follows:

entries.text: Plaintiff serves Second Request for Production
Paralegal sends good-faith letter with copies of requests
Defendant acknowledges receipt; commits to March 26 deadline
Defendant serves supplemental discovery responses and full production via email with Google Drive link
Plaintiff sends second good-faith letter alleging "material deficiency"
Defendant files Motion for Protective Order via PeachCourt

body.text: This record reflects consistent good-faith engagement with the discovery process by a disabled, pro se litigant operating under significant medical and financial constraints.
VI. Regarding Attorney's Fees

Your letter indicates that your client will seek reimbursement of attorney's fees "caused by" my conduct, including deduction from the net proceeds of the marital residence. I note that the appropriateness and amount of any fee award is a matter exclusively within the Court's discretion and must be supported by evidence of actual, reasonable fees incurred as a direct result of sanctionable conduct. Good-faith objections to overbroad discovery do not constitute sanctionable conduct under Georgia law.
VII. Conclusion

I have produced all responsive, non-privileged documents in my possession. I have asserted specific, legally grounded objections where appropriate. I have a pending Motion for Protective Order before the Court. I am in compliance with my discovery obligations.

I remain willing to meet and confer in good faith regarding any specific document or category of documents your office believes was inadequately addressed. I am available by email at willyj1587@gmail.com.

cc: Amy Hilliard Thompson, Paralegal, McGhan Law, LLC
Brenda O'Dell, Designated ADA Support Person
